conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have a child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives with me here in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To help assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few more questions.

Are you responsible for Alex in terms of providing for his day-to-day needs, such as food, clothing, and general care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I take care of all his day-to-day needs - his food, clothes, all of that. He lives at home with me and I look after him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Let me ask you a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

Are you or Alex currently living in the UK, and do you have the right to claim benefits here (for example, are you a UK resident, or do you have settled status or the right to reside in the UK)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, we both live in the UK. I'm a UK resident, so I have the right to claim benefits here.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just one more question to complete my assessment:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or is there anyone else who might also be responsible for him (for example, does he live with anyone else, or spend significant time in someone else's care)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. He lives with me full-time, though I should mention he's actually been in hospital for a couple of months now. But I'm still his parent and responsible for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. I need to understand a bit more about Alex's hospital stay to give you an accurate assessment.

Is Alex expected to be in hospital for more than 12 weeks in total, or has he already been there for more than 12 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been there for 12 weeks exactly. So right at that 12-week mark.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Before I can make a final assessment, I need to read the official guidance on Child Benefit eligibility, particularly regarding children in hospital.

Let me review the rules now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I have one final question to clarify Alex's situation:

Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital (for example, on things like clothes, toiletries, toys, or other items for him)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I still buy things for him - clothes, magazines, his favorite snacks, things like that. I'm still spending money on his needs even though he's in the hospital.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official UK government guidance I've reviewed, let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news - you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!**

Here's why:

✓ Alex is 8 years old (under 16)
✓ He lives in the UK with you
✓ You are responsible for his day-to-day needs
✓ You are a UK resident with the right to claim benefits
✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him

**Important information about Alex's hospital stay:**

While Alex has been in hospital for 12 weeks, the rule states that Child Benefit is only affected if a child is in hospital for **more than 12 weeks**. At exactly 12 weeks, you remain fully entitled.

Even if Alex's stay extends beyond 12 weeks, you would still continue to be eligible because you are regularly spending money on him while he's in hospital (like the clothes, magazines, and snacks you mentioned). This is an exception to the 12-week rule.

If you haven't already claimed Child Benefit, you can do so now. If Alex's hospital stay does extend beyond 12 weeks, you should inform HMRC to ensure your payments continue under the exception.

I hope Alex recovers well!

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), lives in the UK with you as his parent, and you are responsible for his day-to-day needs. While Alex has been in hospital for 12 weeks, according to the official guidance, Child Benefit entitlement is affected only if a child is in hospital for MORE than 12 weeks. At exactly 12 weeks, you remain entitled. Additionally, even if the hospital stay were to exceed 12 weeks, there is an exception to the rule: you can continue to receive Child Benefit if you are regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital, which you have confirmed you are doing (buying clothes, magazines, snacks, etc.). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and you are a UK resident with the right to claim benefits. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. They meet all the basic eligibility criteria: Alex is under 16, lives in the UK, the claimant is responsible for him and lives with him, they are a UK resident with the right to claim benefits, and no one else is claiming for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for 12 weeks, this does not affect eligibility as the threshold is 'more than 12 weeks'. Even if the stay extends beyond 12 weeks, the claimant would still qualify under the exception because they regularly spend money on Alex.

No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and you are a UK resident with the right to claim benefits. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

They meet all the basic eligibility criteria: Alex is under 16, lives in the UK, the claimant is responsible for him and lives with him, they are a UK resident with the right to claim benefits, and no one else is claiming for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for 12 weeks, this does not affect eligibility as the threshold is 'more than 12 weeks'. Even if the stay extends beyond 12 weeks, the claimant would still qualify under the exception because they regularly spend money on Alex.
